title: Listening - Part 4
description: For each question, choose the correct answer.
instructions: You will hear an interview. For questions 20–25, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear.
context: You will hear an interview with a woman called Helen Brooks, who works in a museum.
audio_script: Interviewer: Helen Brooks works in a city museum and organises activities for visitors. Helen, did you study history at university? || Guest: Actually, no. I studied design because I wanted to make exhibitions look interesting. History came later. [20]When I helped create a display about ordinary families from the past, I realised their stories were more interesting than I had expected[/20]. || Interviewer: What is your main job now? || Guest: I plan events for schools and families. People imagine I spend my day looking after old objects, but specialists do that. [21]My job is to turn information into activities that visitors can understand and enjoy[/21]. || Interviewer: What was difficult when you began? || Guest: I used to put too much information into everything. I wanted visitors to know all the details. [22]I had to learn that a short clear story is better than a room full of facts[/22], especially for children. || Interviewer: Which activity has been most successful? || Guest: Last year we ran a night at the museum. I thought the costumes would be the main attraction, but actually [23]families loved using torches to find hidden objects in the dark galleries[/23]. We're doing it again soon. || Interviewer: Do you use technology much? || Guest: Yes, but carefully. Tablets and screens can be useful, though they sometimes stop people looking at real objects. [24]The best technology is the audio guide, because visitors can listen while still moving around[/24]. || Interviewer: What would you like to improve? || Guest: Our museum is popular with schools, but some local teenagers think it isn't for them. [25]I'd like to invite them to design a small exhibition about music and fashion from their own lives[/25]. That could change how they see the place. || Interviewer: Before we finish, Helen, what makes a family visit to a museum successful? || Guest: I think it helps when families don't try to see everything. If children are dragged through every room, they get tired and remember very little. I always suggest choosing three or four things and spending proper time with them. A good conversation in front of one object is better than rushing past a hundred. || Interviewer: Do you have a favourite object in the museum? || Guest: It changes, but at the moment I love an old lunch box from a factory worker. It isn't valuable in the usual way, but it tells you about someone's daily life. Children understand it immediately because they all know what lunch is. From that simple object we can talk about work, food, families and the city. || Interviewer: What is difficult about planning activities? || Guest: The space. Old museum buildings are beautiful, but not always practical. Some rooms echo, some are narrow, and large groups can block doorways. We have to test every activity with real visitors, because something that works on paper may be confusing in a busy gallery. It keeps us humble. || Interviewer: Finally, what do you do when you need new ideas? || Guest: I try to step away from the main work for a short time. Going for a walk, visiting another place or talking to someone outside the job usually helps. If I sit at a desk worrying, nothing improves. I also keep a small notebook, because useful ideas often arrive at inconvenient moments, like on a bus or while making dinner.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Helen Brooks works in a city museum and organises activities for visitors. Helen, did you study history at university?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Actually, no. I studied design because I wanted to make exhibitions look interesting. History came later. When I helped create a display about ordinary families from the past, I realised their stories were more interesting than I had expected.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What is your main job now?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I plan events for schools and families. People imagine I spend my day looking after old objects, but specialists do that. My job is to turn information into activities that visitors can understand and enjoy.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What was difficult when you began?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I used to put too much information into everything. I wanted visitors to know all the details. I had to learn that a short clear story is better than a room full of facts, especially for children.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Which activity has been most successful?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Last year we ran a night at the museum. I thought the costumes would be the main attraction, but actually families loved using torches to find hidden objects in the dark galleries. We're doing it again soon.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do you use technology much?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Yes, but carefully. Tablets and screens can be useful, though they sometimes stop people looking at real objects. The best technology is the audio guide, because visitors can listen while still moving around.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What would you like to improve?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Our museum is popular with schools, but some local teenagers think it isn't for them. I'd like to invite them to design a small exhibition about music and fashion from their own lives. That could change how they see the place.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Before we finish, Helen, what makes a family visit to a museum successful?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I think it helps when families don't try to see everything. If children are dragged through every room, they get tired and remember very little. I always suggest choosing three or four things and spending proper time with them. A good conversation in front of one object is better than rushing past a hundred.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do you have a favourite object in the museum?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: It changes, but at the moment I love an old lunch box from a factory worker. It isn't valuable in the usual way, but it tells you about someone's daily life. Children understand it immediately because they all know what lunch is. From that simple object we can talk about work, food, families and the city.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What is difficult about planning activities?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: The space. Old museum buildings are beautiful, but not always practical. Some rooms echo, some are narrow, and large groups can block doorways. We have to test every activity with real visitors, because something that works on paper may be confusing in a busy gallery. It keeps us humble.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Finally, what do you do when you need new ideas?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I try to step away from the main work for a short time. Going for a walk, visiting another place or talking to someone outside the job usually helps. If I sit at a desk worrying, nothing improves. I also keep a small notebook, because useful ideas often arrive at inconvenient moments, like on a bus or while making dinner.

questions.question: How did Helen become interested in history?

options.B: by working on a display about families

questions.explanation: Helen says creating a display about ordinary families from the past made her interested in their stories.

questions.question: What is Helen's main responsibility at the museum?
questions.explanation: She says her job is to turn information into activities that visitors can understand and enjoy.

questions.question: What did Helen need to learn when she began?

options.B: to write stories for older visitors

questions.explanation: Helen says she learned that a short clear story is better than too many facts.

questions.question: What did families especially enjoy at the night event?
questions.explanation: Helen says families loved using torches to find hidden objects in dark galleries.

questions.question: Why does Helen like the audio guide?

options.A: It helps visitors look at objects while listening.
options.B: It is cheaper than using screens.
options.C: It gives more information than tablets.

questions.explanation: She says visitors can listen to the audio guide while still moving around.

questions.question: What does Helen want local teenagers to do?

options.A: visit more often with their schools
options.B: learn about music from the past

questions.explanation: Helen says she wants teenagers to design a small exhibition about music and fashion from their own lives.
